questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes the *type* required for assigning a Java lambda expression?
options: A lambda can be assigned to any interface type.
A lambda must be assigned to a functional interface (single abstract method).
A lambda requires assignment to an abstract class.
A lambda must implement a concrete class directly.
questions.correct_answer: A lambda must be assigned to a functional interface (single abstract method).

questions.question_text: Select all statements that are true about what Java lambdas can capture or access:
options: A lambda can capture local variables that are *effectively final*.
A lambda can modify a local variable declared outside the lambda.
A lambda can access `final` or instance fields of the enclosing class.
A lambda's `this` refers to the lambda object, not the enclosing instance.
A lambda may throw checked exceptions only if the target functional interface method declares them.
correct_answer: A lambda can capture local variables that are *effectively final*.
A lambda can access `final` or instance fields of the enclosing class.
A lambda may throw checked exceptions only if the target functional interface method declares them.

questions.question_text: At compile time, what determines the *target type* of a lambda expression in Java?
options: The runtime class of the lambda object.
The surrounding context (expected type), such as the variable or parameter type.
The return type of the enclosing method.
A special lambda keyword type inferred by the JVM at runtime.
questions.correct_answer: The surrounding context (expected type), such as the variable or parameter type.

questions.question_text: Which of the following are valid Java method reference forms?
options: `ClassName::staticMethod` (reference to a static method)
`instance::instanceMethod` (reference to a specific instance method)
`ClassName::instanceMethod` (reference to an instance method of an arbitrary object of a particular type)
`ClassName::new` (constructor reference)
`super::method` (reference to a superclass method from outside its class)
correct_answer: `ClassName::instanceMethod` (reference to an instance method of an arbitrary object of a particular type)

questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes modifying local variables from inside a Java lambda?
options: A lambda can freely modify any local variable declared outside it.
A lambda can modify a local variable only if using a `volatile` modifier.
A lambda cannot modify local variables unless they are `final` or *effectively final*.
A lambda can modify local variables if the enclosing method is synchronized.
questions.correct_answer: A lambda cannot modify local variables unless they are `final` or *effectively final*.
